**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I suspect it will find you in the same place you've always been—wherever that is, I dares not guess. Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I suppose it is time I said something, though I fear my words may be too late, or worse, too much.

You were always the child who questioned everything, weren't you? The one who asked why the stars twinkle and why the sea roars when it is so still. I used to think it was a sign of a curious mind, but now I wonder if it was a sign of something else entirely—something I could not see, not then, and not now.

I am a lighthouse keeper, you know. You once called me "Grumpy Grizzle" in your letters, and I suppose I deserved it. I have always been a man of routine, of order, of *control*. The lighthouse is my domain, and I have kept it well—until now.

The storm is coming, and I am not sure if I will survive it. I have not been feeling well for some time, and the sea has been restless. I have been hearing voices in the wind, and I do not know if they are the sea or the devil or something worse. I have been thinking of you often, more than I care to admit.

I have always been a man of few words

I have kept the lantern burning, as I have kept your memory alight in my soul.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth beneath me. I have not written to you in five years, and I have not spoken to you in five years, save for the occasional gruff word to the men who come to mend the lantern or bring provisions. But tonight, the storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can say what I must.

You said you could not stay, that the world was too small for you, that you could not be bound by the sea, by the silence, by the loneliness of this place. I did not argue. I did not beg. I said nothing at all.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it may find you in a far more troubled state than I. It has been five long years since our last words, and I have not penned a single line to you since. I have not even dared to think of you, for fear that such thoughts might bring me to my knees. Yet now, as the tempest gathers, I find myself compelled to write—though I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn.

The sea is restless tonight, and the wind howls as though it were the very devil himself. I have been watching the skies for hours, and the stars have been obscured by a veil of black clouds, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. The lighthouse stands as it always has, steadfast and unyielding, but I fear it may not be enough to keep me from the dark. I have not slept, and I have not eaten, save for a small portion of hardtack and a cup of tea. I do not know if I have the strength to keep this lamp burning through the storm, or if I shall be forced to let it go.
